response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, with its earliest precursor ARPANET first demonstrating inter-institutional connectivity by sending a message between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute in 1969. A string of foundational technical and cultural milestones built the modern internet over the following decades, including the 1983 transition to TCP/IP protocols that created its core technical base, Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 invention of the World Wide Web at CERN, the 1993 launch of the first mainstream web browser Mosaic, the mid-2000s rise of social media, 2006’s launch of cloud computing pioneer AWS, and the 2007 debut of the iPhone that made mobile internet ubiquitous. While the late 1990s dot-com boom brought massive investment in internet startups, most collapsed in the 2001 crash, though survivors Amazon and Google grew to become some of the most valuable companies in history. Today, the internet connects more than 5 billion people worldwide and underpins nearly every core dimension of modern life, spanning commerce, communication, and entertainment.
